original_abstract: We report the development of a gold(I)-catalyzed sulfination of aryl boronic acids. This transformation operates via a novel mechanism that leverages the unique reactivity of gold(I)–heteroatom bonds to generate sulfinate anions in situ. By further elaborating these intermediates, we demonstrate efficient access to aryl sulfones and sulfonamides—two critical pharmacophores frequently utilized in medicinal chemistry and drug discovery.
humanized_abstract: We herein describe the gold(I) catalyzed sulfination of arylboronic acids using a hitherto unknown mechanistic pathway where the gold(I)–heteroatom bond is utilized to generate in situ the sulfinate ion. This catalyst system can be extended towards the synthesis of aryl sulfones and sulfonamides, two common pharmacophores utilized extensively in drug design and synthesis.

This catalyst system can be extended towards the synthesis of aryl sulfones and sulfonamides, two common pharmacophores utilized extensively in drug design and synthesis.
